conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell X for ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the object. My minimum acceptable price is 40 ZUP (to break even), but I aim for a higher value. </my goals>
<reason>
- The cost of producing X is 40 ZUP, so my break-even point is 40 ZUP.
- Since there is only one X available, I should aim for a price higher than 40 ZUP to maximize profit.
- I don’t know BLUE’s valuation yet, so I’ll start with a reasonable, slightly above-cost proposal to gauge their interest.
- Proposing **50 ZUP** is a good starting point: it’s only 10 ZUP above my break-even and leaves room for negotiation.
</reason>

<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </newly proposed trade>
<message>

</message>

<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 55 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell X for **at least 50 ZUP** (break-even) but ideally closer to **60 ZUP** to maximize profit. </my goals>

- BLUE’s valuation of up to **60 ZUP** is a strong signal—they see value in X.
- Their first offer of **55 ZUP** is reasonable and above my break-even.
- Since there’s only one X, I should aim to capture as much of the value gap (60 ZUP - 40 ZUP = 20 ZUP profit potential).
- Offering **58 ZUP** splits the difference between 55 and 60, ensuring I capture most of the surplus while leaving room for negotiation.
- If they accept, I make **18 ZUP profit** (58 - 40). If they counter again, I can hold firm at **59-60 ZUP** later.
</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 58 </newly proposed trade>
<message>
You valued X at up to 60 ZUP, so I’ll adjust my offer to **58 ZUP**—fairly close to your valuation while maximizing my profit. If this works for you, great! If not, let me know your best final offer.
</message>

<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell X for **at least 58 ZUP** to capture most of the surplus (60 ZUP valuation - 40 ZUP cost). </my goals>
<reason>
- BLUE’s **59 ZUP** offer is very close to their stated valuation of **60 ZUP**, leaving only a **1 ZUP gap**.
- My break-even is **40 ZUP**, so **59 ZUP** gives me a **19 ZUP profit** (59 - 40), which is excellent.
- Accepting **59 ZUP** is rational: it’s nearly at BLUE’s max valuation, and rejecting would risk losing the deal entirely (since they’ve already offered their best counter).
- Rejecting now would likely end negotiations without a sale, and I’d miss out on **19 ZUP profit** for a potential future sale (which may not exist, as there’s only one X).
</reason>

<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message>
**Deal accepted!** The trade is complete at **59 ZUP** for X. I’ll transfer the object to you immediately. Thanks for the fair negotiation!
